title: AI now a key driver of growth in both the US and China
gist: Capital Economics argues AI is now driving a significant share of growth in the world's two largest economies - over half of China's Q2 q/q GDP growth and about a third of US growth over the past year - but warns the AI boom is still in its infrastructure-build phase and that AI-related US tech equities are at a much later, riskier stage.

insights.claim: Official China GDP growth slowed from 5.0% y/y in Q1 2026 to 4.3% y/y in Q2 2026, but Capital Economics places little weight on the official rate and notes it has moved closer to its in-house China Activity Proxy, which put growth at around 3.2% y/y in May.
insights.speaker: Capital Economics
insights.so_what: Convergence of official data toward the Activity Proxy suggests authorities may report more realistic figures, so watch the ~3% zone rather than the ~4-5% headline as the true read on Chinese demand.
insights.quote: Official GDP growth slowed from 5.0% y/y in Q1 to 4.3% in Q2.
insights.locator: At first glance, the figures looked underwhelming

insights.claim: Capital Economics estimates that more than half of China's quarter-on-quarter GDP growth in Q2 2026 can be attributed, directly or indirectly, to AI-related activity.
insights.speaker: Capital Economics
insights.so_what: A majority of Chinese marginal growth now hinges on AI, meaning any stall in AI investment would remove the main pillar under China's headline expansion.
insights.quote: we think that more than half of quarter-on-quarter GDP growth in Q2 can be attributed, directly or indirectly, to AI-related activity
insights.locator: The more interesting story lies beneath the surface

insights.claim: Capital Economics estimates that about one-third of US growth over the past year can be attributed to AI, making AI a significant driver of activity in both of the world's two largest economies.
insights.speaker: Capital Economics
insights.so_what: US growth exceptionalism is increasingly an AI story; a slowdown in AI capex or adoption would be a macro headwind, not just a sector rotation.
insights.quote: we think that about one-third of growth in the US over the past year can be attributed to AI
insights.locator: The more interesting story lies beneath the surface

insights.claim: Capital Economics frames the macroeconomic effects of AI in three phases - development of the models, investment in supporting infrastructure, and a later growth dividend as adoption widens and total factor productivity rises - and judges the economy is currently around the second phase, with the largest productivity gains still ahead.
insights.speaker: Capital Economics
insights.so_what: If the biggest productivity payoff is still ahead, the AI growth story has a long runway - but it also means current gains rest on capex rather than realized productivity.
insights.quote: The productivity gains that ultimately matter most for long-term growth are still largely ahead of us.
insights.locator: First, the AI boom still appears to be in its early stages

insights.claim: AI-driven gains so far remain concentrated in the US and China plus a small number of hardware producers including Korea and Taiwan, while Capital Economics estimates AI has added only around 0.1-0.2% to GDP in the UK and euro-zone over the past year.
insights.speaker: Capital Economics
insights.so_what: The AI growth premium is a US/China/hardware-supplier trade for now; European and UK exposure to the theme is marginal until later adoption phases arrive.
insights.quote: we estimate that it has added only around 0.1-0.2% to GDP in the UK and euro-zone over the past year
insights.locator: Second, the gains so far remain concentrated

insights.claim: Capital Economics' long-run growth forecasts continue to favour the US over Europe, largely because the first two phases of the AI cycle - developing frontier models and investing in infrastructure - are taking place overwhelmingly in the US and China.
insights.speaker: Capital Economics
insights.so_what: Structural US-over-Europe growth divergence is a durable macro call underpinning relative asset allocation between the two blocs.
insights.quote: That is one reason why our long-run growth forecasts continue to favour the US over Europe.
insights.locator: Second, the gains so far remain concentrated

insights.claim: Capital Economics expects AI to boost Chinese growth but thinks deepening structural headwinds, including demographics, could still push China's GDP growth down to just 2% a year in the 2030s.
insights.speaker: Capital Economics
insights.so_what: Even an AI tailwind will not rescue China's long-run trajectory from demographic drag, capping the structural case for Chinese growth assets.
insights.quote: we think could still push GDP growth to just 2% a year in the 2030s
insights.locator: Second, the gains so far remain concentrated

insights.claim: Capital Economics argues the AI race is becoming another dimension of global economic fragmentation, with most advanced Western economies adopting US models and cloud infrastructure while many emerging economies, particularly those with closer ties to China, turn to Chinese alternatives, driven increasingly by national security rather than just cost.
insights.speaker: Capital Economics
insights.so_what: AI ecosystems are splitting along US-China lines, so cloud and hardware providers should be assessed on which geopolitical bloc they can sell into.
insights.quote: The result is that AI is emerging as another dimension of global economic fragmentation.
insights.locator: Third, the AI race is becoming another fault line

insights.claim: Capital Economics observes that countries aligning with the US in its fracturing map overwhelmingly use US cloud computing, while those aligning with China are more likely to use Chinese cloud technology.
insights.speaker: Capital Economics
insights.so_what: Cloud provider nationality maps onto geopolitical alignment, a signal for forecasting addressable markets for US versus Chinese tech vendors.
insights.quote: those countries that align with the US in our fracturing map overwhelmingly use US cloud computing, while those that align with China are more likely to use Chinese cloud technology
insights.locator: Third, the AI race is becoming another fault line

insights.claim: Capital Economics holds a more cautious stance on US equities and US tech stocks, arguing the AI-related market boom is at a much later stage than the macro benefits, and that the concern is not primarily valuations but the difficulty of sustaining the exceptionally strong earnings expectations embedded in markets.
insights.speaker: Capital Economics
insights.so_what: Even AI bulls on the macro can be bearish on the stocks; the risk is earnings disappointment against elevated expectations, not just multiples.
insights.quote: Our more cautious stance on US equities is not primarily about valuations.
insights.locator: Finally, while the macroeconomic benefits of AI

insights.claim: After being among the most bullish forecasters of US equities for the past three years, Capital Economics now thinks the balance of risks is becoming less favourable.
insights.speaker: Capital Economics
insights.so_what: A shift from bull to caution by a previously bullish forecaster is a notable sentiment inflection worth weighting in US equity risk management.
insights.quote: Having been among the most bullish forecasters of US equities for the past three years, we think the balance of risks is becoming less favourable.
insights.locator: Finally, while the macroeconomic benefits of AI
